scenario: Nimbus Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 claim settlement parent process that uses a call activity to invoke a child reserves process. After a parent compensation throw event fires, the modelling team needs the child reserves work to be reverted as well across 4500 claims per month.
question: Which approach reverts the effects executed inside the child process when the parent compensation throw event activates?

options.text: A compensation throw event placed in the child process is triggered automatically when the parent compensation throw event fires inside the parent flow scope of the call activity
A compensation boundary event attached to the start event of the child process invokes the child compensation handlers whenever the parent compensation throw event fires later
A compensation intermediate throw event placed inside the parent embedded subprocess invokes the child process compensation handlers via the call activity calledElement reference
A compensation boundary event attached to the call activity in the parent process invokes a parent-side compensation handler that performs the child reversal logic instead

a.text: Incorrect. The Compensating embedded subprocesses section states that compensation handlers of child processes are not invoked from the parent; triggering of compensation stops at the call activity boundary.

b.text: Incorrect. The page states triggering of compensation stops at the call activity, so a boundary event inside the child process is never reached by a parent compensation throw event.

c.text: Incorrect. Compensation is broadcast within its scope and stops at the call activity boundary; it does not cross into the child process via the call activity reference.

d.text: Correct. The Compensating embedded subprocesses section directs you to attach a compensation boundary event on the call activity itself so that a parent-side compensation handler can revert the effects of the child process.

explanation: Compensation triggering does not cross call activity boundaries. To revert effects executed inside a child process, attach a compensation boundary event on the parent's call activity and let its parent-side handler perform the reversal logic.
